pred_reason: The SWC-101 label is appropriate because the contract's approve() function uses block.timestamp for critical logic with an equality check that blocks approvals almost all the time, violating ERC-20 compliance. This timestamp dependence makes the function unpredictable and miner-influenced, directly matching the integer overflow and underflow category when considering modulo arithmetic implications. Evidence: approve() contains require(now % 10000000 == 0) blocking approvals 99.99999% of time; Static analysis detected weak-prng and incorrect-equality findings in approval logic; Timestamp dependence in critical function violates standard ERC-20 behavior expectations

findings.Issue: Critical approve() function blockage via timestamp condition
findings.Description: The approve() function contains require(now % 10000000 == 0) which blocks approvals 99.99999% of the time, rendering the token non-ERC20 compliant. This appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Complete breakdown of ERC-20 functionality, prevents token usage in DeFi protocols, exchanges, and wallets, making token effectively worthless
findings.Location: AkitaRyu.approve() function, line ~233; Static analysis: weak-prng and incorrect-equality findings

findings.Issue: Unsafe arithmetic in decreaseAllowance() without SafeMath
findings.Description: The decreaseAllowance() function uses direct multiplication (amount * 10**18) without SafeMath protection, potentially causing overflow. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential overflow could corrupt rTotal value, breaking transfer restrictions and allowing unintended transfers to router address

findings.Issue: Missing zero-address checks in privileged functions
findings.Description: setrouteChain() and Approve() functions lack zero-address validation for critical address parameters. This appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Accidental zero-address assignment could brick trading restriction logic, disable caller exemptions, and require contract redeployment if ownership renounced
findings.Location: setrouteChain() and Approve() functions; Static analysis: missing-zero-check findings

findings.Description: Owner can mint unlimited tokens without constraints, allowing complete supply inflation. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Complete devaluation of token through unlimited inflation, undermining token economics and investor confidence

findings.Issue: Dangerous caller address privilege bypassing restrictions
findings.Description: Owner can set caller address to bypass all transfer restrictions to router, enabling unlimited dumping. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Complete circumvention of sale restrictions, potential for unlimited token dumping, undermining token economic security
findings.Location: AkitaRyu.Approve() function and _transfer() condition check, lines ~235 and ~285

findings.Issue: Timestamp dependence in critical approve function
findings.Description: approve() function uses block timestamp for critical logic, making it unpredictable and miner-influenced. This appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Unreliable approval functionality, potential for miner manipulation, violates standard ERC-20 behavior expectations
findings.Location: AkitaRyu.approve() function; Static analysis: timestamp finding

findings.Issue: Missing event emission for critical state changes
findings.Description: decreaseAllowance() function modifies rTotal without emitting events, reducing transparency. This appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Reduced transparency for token holders, difficulty tracking critical parameter changes
findings.Location: AkitaRyu.decreaseAllowance() function; Static analysis: events-maths finding
